coredata.dc:title: A feasible and universal one-step method for functionalizing carbon dots efficiently via in-situ free radical polymerization
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Chenghao
Lu, Bin
Wang, Xinyuan
Ma, Yongjun
Ren, Xianyan
Liu, Cailin
Yang, Haijun
coredata.dc:description: The passivation of carbon dots (CDs) are usually implemented through coupling reactions, which requires either specific functional groups on the surface of CDs or multiple steps. In this paper, we propose a feasible method to passivate CDs via in-situ free radical polymerization, which has no dependence on functional groups and thus is suitable for CDs prepared via either bottom-up or top-down route. Taking bare CDs that cut from active carbon by top-down method as an example. When putting acrylic acid, sodium methallyl sulfonate and acrylic phosphate ester as monomers into the bare CDs solution along with ammonium persulfate, stirring at room temperature for 2h, the monomers were polymerized and formed terpolymer-passivated CDs (T-CDs) of which quantum yields (QY) increased obviously. The QY of the bare CDs and the T-CDs were 0.2% and 29.1%, respectively. More interestingly, the bare CDs were found capable of constructing a redox-initiated couple with ammonium persulfate to initiate polymerization. Another example token to further approve the feasibility and universality of “in-situ free radical polymerization” passivation method is our reported poly (N-vinylcarbazole)-passivated CDs (PVK-CDs) that prepared by bottom-up method. The PVK-CDs were synthesized via thermal treatment of a mixture composed of citric acid and N-vinylcarbazole molecules at 300 °C for 2 h. During the process the citric acid was carbonized to provide pristine CDs and N-vinylcarbazole was in-situ polymerized on the surface of the pristine CDs through thermal initiation, which enhances the fluorescence QY up to 40%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Passivation
Free radical polymerization
Redox-initiated couple
Fluorescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Terpolymer passivated CDs that prepared via top-down route
Poly (N-vinylcarbazole) (PVK) passivated CDs that prepared via bottom-up route
The mass ratio of the terpolymer layer to CDs
The surface passivation of bare CDs prepared via top-down route
The surface passivation of CDs prepared via bottom-up route

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The strategy to synthesize T-CDs via top-down route.
Illustration of the synthesis of PVK-CDs via bottom-up route.
(a) The HRTEM picture and size distribution of T-CDs (b) IR spectra of the AA-SMAS-APE and T-CDs (c) 1HNMR spectra of the control sample and T-CDs.
Possible mechanism of the redox-initiated CDs/APS couple and illustration of free radical polymerization.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of T-CDs and T/CDs and PL emission spectra of T-CDs. Insert photo: Left: T/CDs. Right: T-CDs. (b) Changes in fluorescence QY and ratio of terpolymer to CDs corresponding to five groups. Insert photo: T-CDs dispersion in water under UV light.
The cartoon about feasibility and universality of passivation method via free radical polymerization.
Five groups of experiment systems differing the total amount of the monomers.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Preparation of a series of contrasts: AA-SMAS-APE, control sample and T/CDs.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Chemical shifts of 1HNMR spectra of control sample and that of T-CDs.
The comparative results between passivation method based on free radical polymerization and other covalent method.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Abbreviations: EDA: ethylenediamine; DA: dopamine; ACP: activated carbon powder; EDC: 1-ethyl-3-[3-dimethylaminopropyl]carbodiimide NHS: N-hydroxysuccinimide; AC: ammonium citrate; FA: Folic acid; TTDDA: 4,7,10-trioxa-1,13-tridecanediamine; CTA: (3-chloro-2-hydroxypropyl) trimethylammonium chloride; DMAP: 4-dimethylaminopyridine; DCC: N,N′-dicyclohexylcarbodiimide; β-CD: β-cyclodextrin: NVC: N-vinlycarbazole; CA: citric acid.

head.ce:title: A feasible and universal one-step method for functionalizing carbon dots efficiently via in-situ free radical polymerization

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Southwest University of Science and Technology, Mianyang, 621010, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering
Southwest University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Southwest University of Science and Technology, Mianyang, 621010, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The passivation of carbon dots (CDs) are usually implemented through coupling reactions, which requires either specific functional groups on the surface of CDs or multiple steps. In this paper, we propose a feasible method to passivate CDs via in-situ free radical polymerization, which has no dependence on functional groups and thus is suitable for CDs prepared via either bottom-up or top-down route. Taking bare CDs that cut from active carbon by top-down method as an example. When putting acrylic acid, sodium methallyl sulfonate and acrylic phosphate ester as monomers into the bare CDs solution along with ammonium persulfate, stirring at room temperature for 2h, the monomers were polymerized and formed terpolymer-passivated CDs (T-CDs) of which quantum yields (QY) increased obviously. The QY of the bare CDs and the T-CDs were 0.2% and 29.1%, respectively. More interestingly, the bare CDs were found capable of constructing a redox-initiated couple with ammonium persulfate to initiate polymerization. Another example token to further approve the feasibility and universality of “in-situ free radical polymerization” passivation method is our reported poly (N-vinylcarbazole)-passivated CDs (PVK-CDs) that prepared by bottom-up method. The PVK-CDs were synthesized via thermal treatment of a mixture composed of citric acid and N-vinylcarbazole molecules at 300 °C for 2 h. During the process the citric acid was carbonized to provide pristine CDs and N-vinylcarbazole was in-situ polymerized on the surface of the pristine CDs through thermal initiation, which enhances the fluorescence QY up to 40%.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are novel carbon-based nanomaterials with particular small size (diameter below 10 nm) and fluorescence properties that mainly depends on the passivation layer [1]. In 2006, Sun et al. reported that the photoluminescence of CDs could be strongly enhanced after simple surface passivation by diamine-terminated oligomeric poly-(ethylene glycol) [2], which was subsequently developed into prevailing ways of passivation, especially amide coupling reaction. From then on, amino-containing compounds were extensively adopted to passivate and functionalize conventional CDs [3,4]. Due to the features such as facile preparation, low toxicity, good compatibility and environment friendly, CDs as potential candidates have drawn much attention in scientific field. After passivation, the functionalized CDs exhibit more excellent performances and are applied widely as biosensors [5,6], bioimaging [7,8], drug carriers [9,10], photocatalysts [11,12] and photovoltaic devices [13].

There are two strategies to prepare CDs including top-down and bottom-up approach, respectively [14]. In bottom-up approach, small molecules as precursors are carbonized and form the carbon core while some specific functional groups in precursors are kept and link to the surface as passivation layer. In top-down approach, large carbon structures are cut by strong acid or physical method into bare CDs with few functional group on the surface, and therefore is necessary but difficult to be passivated by other organic, inorganic and macro molecules to get better performances [15]. There is a trend that bottom-up approach inclines to produce conventional CDs with a higher fluorescence quantum yield (QY) while top-down method is suitable for preparing bare CDs with a higher carbon content [16].

When preparing functionalized or high-performance CDs via bottom-up route through a one-step process, the passivation layer is restricted to thin growth owing to the harsh reaction conditions. For instance, high temperature is always employed to ensure enough sp2 hybrid carbon atoms in carbon core. During the process, the precursors are partially carbonized to carbon core and meanwhile some stable functional groups are kept on the surface of the carbon core, thereby leading that CDs are functionalized with organic molecules [17,18]. However, as the temperature goes up, the carbonization proceeds continuously and the passivation layer gradually turns into carbon core [19], that is, the formation of carbon core representing the conventional CDs is in conflict with the regulation of passivation layer because low temperature means insufficient CDs while high temperature means the poor passivation layer. As a result, it is hard to keep balance between two aspects while utilizing small molecules to design the core-shell structure of passivated CDs in one step.

Passivating bare CDs prepared via top-down route is of no exception. In traditional coupling reactions, the growth of passivation layer is limited because the surface of the bare CDs cut from large carbon structures are short of functional groups. Notably, bare CDs prepared by most methods are generally not fluorescent or with a low fluorescence QY [20]. To passivate bare CDs, amidation predominates in coupling reactions [21], however, the amidation heavily relies on a few carboxyl groups or amino groups on the surface of bare CDs. Additionally, the amidation should be implemented at a relative high temperature or with the help of coupling agents. Besides, other methods including easterification [22,23] and sulfonylation [24] still require specific functional groups such as hydroxyls or amino groups on the surface of CDs to ensure that the substitution is able to proceed.

For the purpose of passivating CDs efficiently, we propose an in-situ free radical polymerization method which has no dependence on functional groups on the CDs or the critical temperature and therefore is true of all CDs, whether prepared via bottom-up or top-down route. The passivation method is proved feasible and universal when we take CDs that prepared via both bottom-up route and top-down route as examples.

Activated carbon, N-vinylcarbazole, quinine sulfate and phosphorus pentoxide (P2O5) were obtained from Aladdin Industrial Corporation (Shanghai). Ammonium persulfate (APS), hydroxyethyl acrylate (HEA), sodium methallyl sulfonate (SMAS), acrylic acid (AA), citric acid (CA), tetrahydrofuran (THF), o-dichlorobenzene, hexane, methanol, absolute ethanol and nitric acid (HNO3) were purchased from Kelong chemical industry (Chengdu). Dimethylsulfoxide-d6 (DMSO‑d 6) for NMR measurements was provided by Cambridge Isotope Laboratories.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Terpolymer passivated CDs that prepared via top-down route

ce:section.ce:para: Bare CDs were synthesized via typical chemical oxidation method. Briefly, 2.0 g activated carbon was weighted and placed into a three-necked flask, where 50 mL deionized water was added under ultrasonification for totally wetting the activated carbon. Subsequently 50 mL HNO3 was slowly added dropwise in the flask and kept stirring vigorously for 6 h. Thereafter the temperature was raised to 120 °C and refluxed for 48 h. After oxidation, the residue was removed while the supernatant was subjected to the rotary evaporation. The concentrated solution was transferred to a dialysis bag for 24 h and dried in the oven to collect CDs.

Acrylic phosphate ester (APE) was synthesized as a phosphorous-containing monomer. In details, 23.2 g HEA was directly added to the three-necked flask, and 14.2 g P2O5 was slowly added and stirred vigorously in the ice bath at 8 °C. After the addition, the temperature was raised to 75 °C and kept for 3 h. Subsequently, 1.5 mL deionized water was added and kept for 2 h to obtain APE.

Hereafter, the APE, AA and SMAS as monomers polymerized with the existence of the bare CDs. Briefly, under the help of the ultrasonification, 22.8 mg CDs were dispersed to 7.6 mL deionized water resulting a coffee-like brown CDs suspension. 15.0 g APE, 2.4 g AA, and 5.3 g SMAS were firstly dissolved in 15.3 g deionized water as the substrate in a three-neck flask. In the following step, 4.0 mL APS water solution (10 wt%) was added dropwise, likewise, the CDs suspension was added at the same time. After the addition, the reaction was kept at room temperature for 2 h. Finally, the dark brown but clear dispersion containing terpolymer passivated CDs (T-CDs) was obtained (Fig. 1 ).

To further investigate the relationship between terpolymer passivation layer and bare CDs, four other experiments using the same amount of bare CDs but different total amount of monomers were designed as listed in Table 1 . Herein, absolute ethanol was applied to dissolve unreacted monomers and precipitate T-CDs in a beaker. After removing the solution, the T-CDs are redissolved in 10 mL deionized water and precipitated by ethanol again. The same process undergoes for three times to purify the T-CDs which are subsequently dried for further use.

A terpolymer AA-SMAS-APE was synthesized using AA, SMAS and APE as monomers which were thermally initiated by APS at 80 °C for 2 h. The AA-SMAS-APE was synthesized as a standard to determine whether the AA, SMAS and APE polymerize on the surface of the CDs and produce the terpolymer passivation layer.

A control sample was prepared following the same process as that of T-CDs at room temperature for 2h. The control sample was prepared as a contrast to demonstrate that with the CDs are existence the monomers AA, SMAS and APS can polymerize at room temperature due to a CDs/APS redox-initiated couple.

A mixture (T/CDs) in which the concentration of the CDs is as the same as that in the T-CDs was prepared. In brief, under the help of the ultrasonification 22.8 mg of the CDs were dispersed to 7.6 mL of the deionized water resulting a coffee-like brown CDs suspension. Then the CDs suspension was directly added to the as-prepared AA-SMAS-APE under stirring. The mixture T/CDs was prepared as a contrast to T-CDs with a purpose of demonstrating their intrinsic differences.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Poly (N-vinylcarbazole) (PVK) passivated CDs that prepared via bottom-up route
ce:section.ce:para: The procedure to synthesis PVK passivated CDs (PVK-CDs) that prepared via bottom-up route has been reported by our group [25]. Briefly, the PVK-CDs were synthesized via thermal treatment of a mixture composed of citric acid and N-vinyl carbazole molecules at 300 °C for 2 h. During the process the citric acid was carbonized to provide pristine CDs and N-vinylcarbazole polymerized on the surface of the pristine CDs through thermal-initiated free radical polymerization.

In typical procedures, 0.96 g CA powder was pre-carbonized at about 200 °C for several minutes which then dispersed into 10 mL THF under ultrasonification for 5 min together with 1.93 g N-vinylcarbazole to form a transparent suspension. The obtained suspension was subsequently concentrated and dried. Thereafter, the dried solid was put into the furnace and treated at 300 °C for 2 h. After the process in furnace, the products were dispersed in 5 mL THF with vigorous sonication and then precipitated into 25 mL hot hexane and 25 mL hot methanol in sequence. Finally, the precipitates were dispersed in o-dichlorobenzene with sonication for 30 min and the resulting dispersion was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min to remove any insoluble or agglomerated larger particles. The obtained dark colored supernatant was that contains PVK-CDs (Fig. 2 ).

High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) was used to take pictures of the morphology by Zeiss Libra 200 FE. Infrared (IR) spectra was obtained by a Bruker TENSOR 27 infrared spectrometer. A Bruker Advance 500 NMR spectrometer was used to carry out the nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) analysis. The ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were measured on a Shimadzu U-3900 UV–vis spectrophotometer and the photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra were recorded on a Perkin Elmer LS55 PL spectrometer.

In brief, quinine sulfate (QYS = 0.55) in 0.1 M H2SO4 (η = 1.33) was taken as a reference and the absorbance was firstly measured by UV–visible absorption spectrometer. The fluorescence QY of T-CDs was calculated according to the following equation:
QY = QY
S ·(F/F
S )·(A
S /A)·(η/η
S )2

Here, A, η and F represent the absorbance at the excitation wavelength, the refractive index of the solvent and the integrated emission intensity calculated from the area under the emission peak on the same wavelength scale, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mass ratio of the terpolymer layer to CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The dried T-CDs were weighed and the mass ratio of the terpolymer layer to CDs was calculated according to the following equation:
Mass ratio (Terpolymer:CDs)=(m-m
0
)/m
0
Where m and m0 represent the mass of the produced T-CDs and that of the CDs added in the system, respectively, and therefore (m-m0) represents the mass of the terpolymer in the T-CDs.

Top-down approach enables large carbon structures to split into great deal of nano scaled CDs with high carbon contents but limited functional groups leading to very weak fluorescence. Another reason accounting for poor fluorescence QY is that non-passivated bare CDs could easily get aggregation, resulting in fluorescence quenching. Consequently, to enhance the emission by passivating CDs with more functional groups via prevailing coupling reactions seems to be essential. However, bare CDs prepared via top-down route only possess a small number of functional groups, which can hardly meet the demands of the coupling reactions.

Although it is free to select carbon sources which are abundant in amino groups or carboxyl groups so that CDs via bottom-up method could still remain these functional groups for further functionalization, the conflict between the regulation of passivation layer and the growth of carbon core also emerges. Taking the amidation as an example, the picked carbon sources always possess carboxyl groups or amino groups for amide coupling reactions [26]. However, in a one-step process, the insufficient temperature means a thick passivation layer and a small carbon core while the excessive carbonization results in a poor passivation layer with an adequate carbon core. Furthermore, some factors such as steric hindrance or reaction condition should be taken in consideration. For instance, the coupling reactions are strict with solvents, temperature and atmosphere, otherwise the yield is hard to promise, or even the reaction is difficult to proceed.

Thus, looking for a facile and effective way to passivate bare CDs is essential and necessary. In-situ free radical polymerization directly enabled passivation layer to modify the surface of bare CDs in a one-step process. Differing from the typical coupling reactions, in-situ free radical polymerization has several benefits such as mild reaction conditions, independent on specific functional groups and variety of monomers. It is of great significance that in-situ free radical polymerization is suitable for passivating CDs prepared in different approaches and started from different precursors. Furthermore, the fluorescence of polymer-passivated CDs is tunable by controlling the passivation layer.

To study the feasibility and universality of the radical polymerization to functionalize CDs, we adopted thermal-initiated and redox-initiated polymerization to passivate CDs from top-down route and bottom-up route, respectively. Besides, monomers not only possessing unsaturated carbon bonds but also containing heteroatoms such as nitrogen, sulfur, phosphorous are adopted to form the passivation layer, due to that the emission of the CDs is sensitive to the thickness and the composition of the layer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The surface passivation of bare CDs prepared via top-down route
ce:section.ce:para: After refluxing in HNO3, activated carbon powder as carbon source was totally oxidized and cut into nanosized based bare CDs. Monomers AA, APE and SMAS easily polymerized on the surface of the CDs and meanwhile introducing heteroatoms such as phosphorus and sulfur to passivation layer. This terpolymer passivated CDs were named as T-CDs. In a representative image shown in Fig. 3 a, individual sizes of T-CDs mostly in the range of 3–6 nm in diameter, consistent with expected slight size increases associated with the passivation of the starting bare CDs by polymer layer.

The IR spectra and 1HNMR spectra of the T-CDs further prove that monomers AA, APE and SMAS polymerized on the surface of the bare CDs. As shown in Fig. 3b, the T-CDs show almost the same peaks as that belonging to the AA-SMAS-APE. The peaks at 1043 cm−1 and 1722 cm−1 belong to C–O and CO stretch vibration of carboxyl groups from AA and APE; The peaks at 1043 cm−1 and 1196 cm−1 are attributed to stretch vibration of S–O and SO groups from SMAS; The peaks at 626 cm−1 and 1196 cm−1 belong to bending vibration of P–O and stretch vibration of PO from APE, respectively. Additionally, the broad peak around 2967 cm−1 is assigned to stretch vibration of C–H in –CH2 and –CH3, which is in side groups of SMAS and APE respectively.

The 1HNMR are shown in Fig. 3c and all primary assignments are listed in Table 2 . In the 1HNMR spectra of control sample, apart from the peaks of DMSO‑d 6 and ethanol (washing solution), peaks around δ 1.82 ppm and δ 3.18 ppm are attributed to –CH3 (peak f) and –CH2-SO3 - (peak g) in side groups of SMAS. The proton signals at δ 4.75 ppm (peak d) and δ 4.79 (peak c) ppm correspond to –COO–CH2-CH2-O-PO3 2- in APE [27].

Obviously, the peaks of c, d, f and g in the control sample are almost disappeared in the 1HNMR spectra of the T-CDs, indicating that due to the existence of bare CDs in the core, the vibration relaxations of side groups in outer passivation layer are restricted by large nanoparticles like bare CDs. This similar phenomenon was also reported in our another work [28].

Moreover, in the 1HNMR spectra of the T-CDs, the broad peaks from 3.52 ppm to 3.60 ppm and 1.30 ppm 1.60 ppm attribute to -C–CH2– (peak e) and –CH2-CH- (peak a, b) in the backbone of the terpolymer, demonstrating that AA, SMAS and APE polymerized even under room temperature with the existence of the CDs. As a contrast, when AA, SMAS and APE polymerized under room temperature for 2 h without the existence of the CDs, only little terpolymer can be collected. And as shown In Fig. 3c, the integrated peak area from 0 to δ 5 ppm of T-CDs is 450.75, whereas that of control sample is 232.91. The reason could be that the reductive bare CDs are able to construct a redox-initiated couple with oxidative APS, and thus initiate the polymerization at room temperature, boosting the degree of polymerization.

The possible mechanism that the monomers are capable of being initiated and polymerized at room temperature is depicted in Fig. 4 . In the CDs/APS redox-initiated couple, CDs serve as both electron acceptors and electron donors [29]. Firstly, CDs provide electrons to APS and render the S2O8 2− to break and release SO4 2− along with SO4 •- radicals that initiate the polymerization. Meanwhile, the SO4 •- radicals add to the carbon-carbon double bonds of three monomers for propagation. Consequently, the interactions of S2O8 2− and CDs make it possible to lower the decomposition activation energy of APS, prompting the redox-initiated polymerization.

The optical properties further prove the feasibility of the in-situ free radical polymerization in passivation on the surface of bare CDs. From the observation of Fig. 5 a, on the one hand, compared with the T-CDs which is a clear dispersion, T/CDs that prepared by simply mixing the CDs suspension with the prepared AA-SMAS-APE solution with the help of ultrasonification is still an unstable suspension, in which the CDs will precipitate immediately (Insert photo). Thus, unlike the T-CDs, the T/CDs exhibit very weak or negligible visible absorptions in Fig. 5a. On the other hand, the emission of T-CDs exhibit apparent excitation wavelength dependence, which is a typical characteristic of CDs ever reported [30] as the excitation wavelength ranges from 400 nm to 540 nm with a step of 20 nm. Consequently, we can say that in T-CDs, the bare CDs participated in polymerization and were passivated by the resulted terpolymer rather than simply absorbed by the terpolymer.

Reasonably, the fluorescence QY of T-CDs is times higher than that of the bare CDs and can be regulated by adjusting the thickness of the passivation layer. Here, the thickness of the terpolymer passivation layer was manipulated by changing the total amount of the monomers. By measuring the fluorescence QY and calculating the mass ration of the terpolymer layer to CDs, as shown in Fig. 5b, it is remarkable that with the dosage of the monomers increasing, not only the mass ratio but also the fluorescence QY is increased. The increase of the mass ratio represents the increase of the thickness of the passivation layer. It is the passivation layer that makes the QY of bare CDs increased from about 0.2% [31,32] to about 29.1%. In another word, a certain amount of terpolymer contributes to enhance the fluorescence of T-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The surface passivation of CDs prepared via bottom-up route
ce:section.ce:para: To further clarify the feasibility and universality of free radical polymerization, our previous work about PVK-CDs was cited here [25] so as to prove that free radical polymerization was also suitable for pristine CDs prepared by bottom-up method.

A one-step thermal-initiated free radical polymerization was designed to promise the growth of carbon core and the formation of passivation layer in order to passivate CDs where citric acid was sacrificed as pristine CDs and N-vinylcarbazole acted as the monomer. The morphology, solubility and optical properties demonstrated the successful preparation of PVK-CDs, that is, in-situ free radical polymerization enables PVK to form a passivation layer around CDs. Besides, the passivation layer not only dramatically increase the fluorescence QY up to 40%, but also change the solvent affinity and endow new functions to the bare CDs. For instance, the PVK passivation layer gives good organic solubility and improves photoelectron conversion ability to CDs [33].

A one-step method for functionalizing CDs via in-situ free radical polymerization was proposed and its feasibility and universality were demonstrated by taking two kinds of CDs prepared by bottom-up route and top-down route, respectively, as examples. Bare CDs in top-down approach can be easily passivated by in-situ polymerized AA, SMAS and APS. The resulted T-CDs reached the fluorescence QY of about 29.1%, which was many times more than that of bare CDs. In our reported bottom-up approach, the one-step free radical polymerization process was also successfully applied in PVK-CDs with the enhancement of fluorescence QY up to 40%.

Differing from other prevailing coupling reactions which heavily relies on specific groups of CDs, in-situ free radical polymerization is free from concerns above and monomers could be chosen or designed flexibly to passivate CDs in order to endow CDs with diverse properties and functions. Hence, free radical polymerization provided inspirations in synthesizing desirable polymer-passivated CDs. And importantly, CDs exhibiting excellent electron-accepting and electron-donating properties made it possible to interact with peroxide initiator, promising successful redox-initiated polymerization under ambient conditions.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We appreciate the financial support of the Startup Foundation for Doctors [16ZX7139] and the Longshan Talent Program [17LZX675, 18LZXT01] by the Southwest University of Science and Technology. The authors also thank their coworkers who participated in this work.
